questions.question:
Which of the following statements is true?
A. ROM is a Read/Write memory
B. PC points to the last instruction that was executed
C. Stack works on the principle of LIFO
D. All instructions affect the flags
questions.answer: C. Stack works on the principle of LIFO
questions.to_find: Which statement about computer architecture components is correct

understand.plain: This question tests basic knowledge of fundamental computer architecture components: ROM, Program Counter (PC), Stack, and condition flags. Each option makes a claim about one of these components, and you need to identify the one true statement.

keywords.explain: Memory that can only be read, not written to during normal operation — it is pre-programmed and retains data without power.
keywords.example: BIOS firmware is stored in ROM chips.

keywords.explain: A data structure principle where the last element inserted is the first one to be removed, like a stack of plates.
keywords.example: Pushing A, B, C onto a stack and popping gives C, B, A in that order.

keywords.explain: A special register that holds the address of the NEXT instruction to be fetched and executed, not the last one.
keywords.example: If instruction at address 100 is being executed, PC typically holds 104 (next instruction).

keywords.explain: Status bits (Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign) that are updated by arithmetic/logic instructions to reflect the result.
keywords.example: A MOV instruction typically does NOT affect flags, while ADD does.

understand.visual_alt: Four options about ROM, PC, Stack, and flags — checking which architectural fact is correct.

given.aim: Identify the true statement about basic computer architecture components.

terms.meaning: Read-Only Memory — cannot be written to during normal operation
terms.connects: Option A claims ROM is Read/Write — this is FALSE

terms.meaning: Register holding address of NEXT instruction to execute
terms.example: PC = 104 when instruction at 100 is executing
terms.connects: Option B claims PC points to LAST instruction — this is FALSE

terms.meaning: LIFO data structure — push/pop from the top only
terms.example: Function call stack uses LIFO discipline
terms.connects: Option C claims stack uses LIFO — this is TRUE

terms.meaning: Condition codes set by certain instructions (not all)
terms.example: MOV instruction does not change flags; ADD does
terms.connects: Option D claims ALL instructions affect flags — this is FALSE

given.plan: Evaluate each option against known computer architecture facts. ROM is read-only (not read/write). PC holds the address of the NEXT instruction (not last). Stack uses LIFO — this is correct by definition. Not all instructions affect flags (e.g., data movement instructions typically do not).

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: ROM is Read/Write memory
steps.apply: ROM stands for Read-Only Memory. By definition it cannot be written to during normal operation. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: RAM (Random Access Memory) is Read/Write, not ROM.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B: PC points to last instruction executed
steps.apply: The Program Counter (PC) always holds the address of the NEXT instruction to be fetched. After an instruction at address X (length L bytes) is fetched, PC = X + L. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: PC is updated during the fetch phase to point to the next instruction.

steps.title: Evaluate Option C: Stack works on principle of LIFO
steps.apply: A stack is a fundamental data structure where the last element pushed is the first element popped — Last In, First Out (LIFO). This is the defining property of a stack. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: LIFO is the correct and universally accepted characterization of a stack.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D: All instructions affect the flags
steps.apply: Only certain instructions affect condition flags (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow). Data transfer instructions like MOV, load/store, and branch instructions typically do NOT modify flags. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: In most ISAs (x86, ARM, RISC), only arithmetic and logical operations update flags.

solution.result: Option C — Stack works on the principle of LIFO — is the only true statement.

questions.formula_note: Basic definitions of ROM (read-only), PC (points to next instruction), Stack (LIFO), and condition flags (not set by all instructions).

questions.question:
Which of the following is NOT a form of memory?
A. Instruction cache
B. Instruction register
C. Instruction opcode
D. Translation look-aside buffer
questions.to_find: Which option among instruction cache, instruction register, instruction opcode, and TLB is NOT a memory component

understand.plain: The question asks you to identify which option is not a type or form of memory storage in a computer. Three of the options are actual hardware memory structures used in processors, while one is just a field within an instruction, not a memory structure.

keywords.explain: A small, fast memory that stores recently fetched instructions to speed up repeated instruction fetches.
keywords.example: L1 instruction cache in modern CPUs holds recently used machine code.

keywords.explain: The binary code within an instruction that specifies the operation to perform — it is a FIELD in an instruction, not a memory unit.
keywords.example: In ADD R1, R2, R3 — the opcode bits specify 'ADD'; it is not a memory component.

keywords.explain: A cache memory that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations to speed up memory access.
keywords.example: When a program accesses virtual address 0x1000, TLB quickly provides the corresponding physical address.

keywords.explain: A register (a small form of memory) that holds the currently fetched instruction being decoded and executed.
keywords.example: After fetching from memory, the 32-bit instruction word is stored in IR.

understand.visual_alt: Four options — instruction cache (memory), instruction register (memory), instruction opcode (not memory, just a field), TLB (memory).

given.aim: Identify the option that is NOT a form of memory.

terms.meaning: Fast on-chip memory storing recently fetched instructions
terms.connects: This IS a form of memory — eliminates A

terms.meaning: A register holding the current instruction — registers ARE a form of memory
terms.connects: This IS a form of memory — eliminates B

terms.meaning: A bit-field within an instruction encoding the operation type — not a memory structure
terms.connects: This is NOT a memory structure — correct answer

terms.meaning: A specialized cache for virtual-to-physical address translation entries
terms.connects: This IS a form of memory — eliminates D

given.plan: Classify each option as a memory structure or not. Cache, registers, and TLB are all hardware storage (memory). Opcode is just a bit-field that encodes the operation, not a physical memory unit.

steps.title: Check Instruction Cache
steps.apply: Instruction cache is a dedicated on-chip SRAM that stores recently used instructions. It is clearly a form of memory (a cache memory). Option A is a form of memory.
steps.note: Caches are a fundamental level of the memory hierarchy.

steps.title: Check Instruction Register
steps.apply: The Instruction Register (IR) is a hardware register in the CPU datapath that temporarily holds the currently executing instruction. Registers are the fastest and smallest form of memory. Option B is a form of memory.
steps.note: Registers are at the top of the memory hierarchy.

steps.title: Check Instruction Opcode
steps.apply: An opcode (operation code) is a binary bit-field within an instruction word that encodes the operation (ADD, SUB, LOAD, etc.). It is part of the instruction FORMAT or ENCODING, not a physical memory structure or storage unit. Option C is NOT a form of memory.
steps.note: Opcode is a design abstraction / encoding field, not a hardware memory component.

steps.title: Check Translation Look-aside Buffer
steps.apply: A TLB is a specialized associative cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations. It is an associative memory structure used by the MMU. Option D is a form of memory.
steps.note: TLBs are considered content-addressable memory (CAM) structures.

solution.result: Option C — Instruction opcode — is NOT a form of memory; it is a bit-field in an instruction encoding.

questions.formula_note: Memory hierarchy classification: registers, cache (L1/L2), TLB, main memory — opcode is an instruction field, not a memory structure.

questions.question:
Consider a new instruction named branch-on-bit-set (mnemonic bbs). The instruction "bbs reg, pos, label" jumps to label if bit in position pos of register operand reg is one. A register is 32-bits wide and the bits are numbered 0 to 31, bit in position 0 being the least significant. Consider the following emulation of this instruction on a processor that does not have bbs implemented:

    tmp= reg & mask
    if (tmp != 0) goto label

The variable tmp is a temporary register. For correct emulation, the variable mask must be generated as:
A. mask = -0x1 << pos
B. mask = -(0xffffffff << pos)
C. mask = 0x1 << pos
D. mask = -(0x1 << pos)
questions.to_find: The correct expression for mask that makes the bbs instruction emulation work

understand.plain: The bbs instruction checks if a specific bit at position pos in a register is 1. The emulation uses a bitwise AND with a mask, then checks if the result is non-zero. We need a mask that has only bit pos set to 1 and all other bits set to 0, so that AND with reg isolates just that one bit.

keywords.explain: Using AND with a mask to isolate specific bits. If mask has only bit k set, then reg & mask is non-zero if and only if bit k of reg is 1.
keywords.example: reg = 0b1010, mask = 0b0010 (bit 1 set). reg & mask = 0b0010 != 0, so bit 1 is set.

keywords.explain: Shifting a value left by n positions multiplies by 2^n and effectively places a 1-bit at position n.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="160" viewBox="0 0 480 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="460" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="35" class="lbl ink">mask = 0x1 << pos  (pos=2 example)</text><rect x="10" y="65" width="460" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="90" class="lbl ink">mask = 0000...0100  (only bit 2 is set)</text><rect x="10" y="115" width="460" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="140" class="lbl ink">reg & mask != 0  iff  bit 2 of reg = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing mask = 0x1 << pos sets only bit at position pos. ANDing with reg isolates that bit — non-zero result means the bit is set.

given.aim: Find the mask expression that isolates bit at position pos in a 32-bit register.

terms.meaning: Jump to label if bit pos of reg is 1
terms.example: bbs R1, 3, L1 — jump if bit 3 of R1 is set
terms.connects: We emulate this by computing reg & mask and checking if result != 0

terms.meaning: A 32-bit value with exactly one bit set at position pos
terms.connects: reg & mask is non-zero if and only if bit pos of reg is 1

terms.meaning: Shift the value 1 left by pos positions — places a 1-bit at position pos, 0s elsewhere
terms.connects: This creates the exact isolating mask needed

given.plan: We want mask with only bit pos set to 1. The expression 0x1 << pos shifts a single 1-bit into position pos, giving exactly this mask. Verify: reg & (0x1 << pos) != 0 iff bit pos of reg is 1.

steps.title: Determine what mask must look like
steps.apply: For the emulation `tmp = reg & mask; if (tmp != 0) goto label` to correctly check bit pos, mask must have ONLY bit pos set to 1. Then reg & mask isolates only that bit — the result is non-zero exactly when bit pos of reg is 1.
steps.note: Any extra bits set in mask would cause false positives if those bits are set in reg.

steps.title: Evaluate each candidate
steps.apply:
Option A: mask = -0x1 << pos. -0x1 in two's complement is 0xFFFFFFFF. Shifting left by pos gives 0xFFFFFFFF << pos, which has MULTIPLE bits set — too broad, incorrect.
Option B: mask = -(0xffffffff << pos). This is a complement of a shifted all-ones, resulting in a value with bits 0 to pos-1 set — also not just bit pos.
Option C: mask = 0x1 << pos. This sets exactly one bit at position pos — correct.
Option D: mask = -(0x1 << pos). Negating 2^pos gives a two's complement value with multiple bits set — incorrect.
steps.note: Option C is the classic bit-isolation mask used in all bit manipulation contexts.

steps.title: Verify with an example
steps.apply: Let pos = 2, reg = 0b00000101 (decimal 5). mask = 0x1 << 2 = 0b00000100. tmp = 0b00000101 & 0b00000100 = 0b00000100 != 0. Bit 2 of reg is indeed 1 (reg = 5 = 4+1, bit 2 is set). Emulation is correct.
steps.note: Also check: reg = 0b00000011, pos = 2. mask = 0b100. tmp = 0b011 & 0b100 = 0. Bit 2 of 3 is 0. Correct — no branch.

solution.result: Option C — mask = 0x1 << pos — is the correct expression for isolating bit at position pos.

questions.formula_note: Bit isolation mask: 0x1 << pos creates a value with only bit pos set; ANDing with a register and checking != 0 tests that specific bit.

questions.question:
Consider a RISC machine where each instruction is exactly 4 bytes long. Conditional and unconditional branch instructions use PC-relative addressing mode with Offset specified in bytes to the target location of the branch instruction. Further the Offset is always with respect to the address of the next instruction in the program sequence. Consider the following instruction sequence:

  Instr. No.    Instruction
  i             add R2, R3, R4
  i+1           sub R5, R6, R7
  i+2           cmp R1, R9, R10
  i+3           beq R1, R0, Offset

If the target of the branch instruction is i, then the decimal value of the Offset is ___
questions.to_find: The decimal value of the PC-relative offset for the branch at instruction i+3 targeting instruction i

understand.plain: In this RISC machine, each instruction takes 4 bytes of memory. The branch instruction uses PC-relative addressing where the offset is relative to the NEXT instruction after the branch (i.e., instruction i+4). We need to calculate how many bytes to go back from instruction i+4 to reach instruction i.

keywords.explain: The branch target address is computed as (PC of next instruction) + Offset, where Offset can be positive (forward) or negative (backward).
keywords.example: If next PC = 100 and we want to jump to address 84, Offset = 84 - 100 = -16.

keywords.explain: Instruction i has some base byte address. Instruction i+k has byte address (base + 4k) since each instruction is 4 bytes.
keywords.example: If i is at byte 200, then i+1 is at 204, i+2 at 208, i+3 at 212, i+4 at 216.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="440" height="220" viewBox="0 0 440 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="200" height="36" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="33" class="lbl ink">i: addr = base</text><rect x="10" y="52" width="200" height="36" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="75" class="lbl ink">i+1: addr = base+4</text><rect x="10" y="94" width="200" height="36" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="117" class="lbl ink">i+2: addr = base+8</text><rect x="10" y="136" width="200" height="36" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="159" class="lbl ink">i+3: beq (base+12)</text><rect x="10" y="178" width="200" height="36" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="201" class="mut lbl">i+4: next (base+16)</text><text x="230" y="75" class="lbl ink">Target = base</text><text x="230" y="105" class="lbl ink">Next PC = base+16</text><text x="230" y="145" class="lbl ink">Offset = base-(base+16)</text><text x="230" y="175" class="lbl ink">= -16</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing instructions i through i+4 at byte offsets 0, 4, 8, 12, 16 from base. Branch at i+3 (base+12), next instruction at base+16, target at base. Offset = base - (base+16) = -16.

given.aim: Calculate the PC-relative byte offset for a backward branch from instruction i+3 to instruction i.

terms.meaning: Each instruction is exactly 4 bytes long
terms.example: Instructions i, i+1, i+2, i+3 occupy bytes 0-3, 4-7, 8-11, 12-15 from base
terms.connects: Determines byte addresses of each instruction

terms.meaning: beq is at instruction i+3, which is at byte address base + 3*4 = base + 12
terms.example: If base = 1000, beq is at byte address 1012
target = base, next_PC = base+16, so Offset = base - (base+16) = -16
terms.connects: This is the decimal value asked for

given.plan: Assign byte addresses relative to instruction i (base = 0 for simplicity). Each instruction is 4 bytes. The branch at i+3 is at byte 12; the next sequential instruction i+4 is at byte 16. The target is instruction i at byte 0. Offset = target_byte - next_PC_byte = 0 - 16 = -16.

steps.title: Assign byte addresses to each instruction
steps.apply:
Let base = address(i). Then:
  i   -> base + 0
  i+1 -> base + 4
  i+2 -> base + 8
  i+3 -> base + 12   (this is the beq instruction)
  i+4 -> base + 16   (next sequential instruction after branch)
steps.note: Each instruction is 4 bytes, so consecutive instructions differ by 4 in their byte addresses.

steps.title: Identify target address and next PC
steps.apply:
The branch instruction is at address base+12. The next instruction in sequence is at base+16. The target of the branch is instruction i at address base.
Offset = base - (base + 16) = -16.
steps.note: Offset is negative because we are branching BACKWARD (to an earlier instruction).

steps.title: Verify the branch target calculation
steps.apply: branch_target = (base + 16) + (-16) = base. This is indeed instruction i. The offset -16 is correct.
steps.note: PC-relative offset of -16 means go back 16 bytes (= 4 instructions) from the next instruction's address.

solution.result: The decimal value of the Offset is -16.

questions.formula_note: PC-relative branch addressing: target_address = PC_of_next_instruction + Offset; here Offset = 0 - 16 = -16 bytes (backward branch spanning 4 instructions of 4 bytes each).

questions.question:
Using an expanding opcode encoding for instructions, is it possible to encode all of the following in an instruction format shown below:
  14 double address instructions
  127 single address instructions
  60 zero address instructions

The instruction format has:
  <- 4 bits -> | <- 6 bits -> | <- 6 bits ->
  Opcode        Operand 1      Operand 2

Justify your answer.
questions.to_find: Whether expanding opcode can encode 14 double-address, 127 single-address, and 60 zero-address instructions in a 4+6+6 bit instruction format

understand.plain: Expanding opcode is a technique where short opcodes are used for instructions needing more operand bits, and longer opcodes are used for instructions needing fewer operand bits. The total instruction word is fixed-length (here 16 bits: 4+6+6). We need to check if we can fit 14 two-operand, 127 one-operand, and 60 zero-operand instructions within this format.

keywords.explain: A scheme where the opcode field can 'expand' into operand fields for instructions that need fewer operands. Some opcode patterns are reserved as prefix codes to indicate the instruction uses more opcode bits.
keywords.example: In a 4-bit opcode, patterns 0000-1101 (14 values) can encode 14 double-address instructions; pattern 1110 expands into the first 6-bit field for single-address; 1111 expands further for zero-address.

keywords.explain: With n opcode bits, there are 2^n possible patterns. Each pattern used for double-address instructions cannot be used for expanding (single or zero address).
keywords.example: 4-bit opcode gives 16 patterns. Using 14 for double-address leaves 2 patterns for expansion.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="200" viewBox="0 0 480 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="460" height="40" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="35" class="lbl ink">Double-addr: 14 opcodes use 4-bit codes (0000 to 1101)</text><rect x="10" y="58" width="460" height="40" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="83" class="lbl ink">Single-addr: expand with 1110 + 6-bit opcode (64 codes)</text><rect x="10" y="106" width="460" height="40" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="131" class="lbl ink">  But need 127 single-addr: use 1110+6bits (64) + 1111+6bits(64)=128 >= 127</text><rect x="10" y="154" width="460" height="40" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="179" class="lbl ink">Zero-addr: use 1111+111111 prefix, then last 6 bits = 64 codes >= 60</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Expanding opcode layout: 14 double-address use 4-bit opcodes 0000-1101. 1110 prefix expands into Operand1 field for single-address (64 codes). 1111 prefix expands further — after reserving space for 127 single-address and 60 zero-address, we check if the totals fit within available code space.

given.aim: Determine if expanding opcode can encode 14 + 127 + 60 instructions in a 16-bit (4+6+6) format.

terms.connects: Defines the bit budget available for opcode expansion

terms.meaning: Use full 4-bit opcode, both operand fields used as addresses
terms.connects: Uses 14 of 16 = 2^4 patterns; leaves 2 patterns (1110, 1111) for expansion

terms.meaning: Use 4-bit opcode field + 6-bit Operand1 field as extended opcode, Operand2 used as address
terms.example: Opcode prefix 1110 + 6-bit extended opcode = 64 codes; prefix 1111 + 6-bit = 64 more = 128 total capacity
terms.connects: 128 available >= 127 needed — but we need to share the 1111 prefix with zero-address

terms.example: Prefix 1111 + 6-bit extended opcode1; then remaining patterns in the second 6-bit field give zero-address codes
terms.connects: After allocating single-address instructions, remaining patterns cover zero-address

given.plan: Allocate opcode patterns systematically. Use 4-bit codes 0000-1101 for 14 double-address instructions. Use prefix 1110 + 6 bits for 64 single-address instructions. Use prefix 1111 + 6 bits for another 64 single-address (need 127-64=63 more; use 63 of 64 patterns). Reserve the remaining 1 pattern (1111 111111) with its 6-bit Operand2 field for 64 zero-address instructions (>= 60 needed). Check if this works out.

steps.title: Allocate double-address instruction opcodes
steps.apply: Total 4-bit patterns = 2^4 = 16. Assign 14 patterns (0000 through 1101) to the 14 double-address instructions. This leaves patterns 1110 and 1111 available as expansion prefixes.
steps.note: We need exactly 14, and we have 14 patterns reserved — no waste.

steps.title: Allocate single-address instruction opcodes
steps.formula_raw: Single-address capacity with prefix X = 2^6 = 64 (if entire first operand field used as opcode extension)
steps.apply:
Using expansion prefix 1110: combine with 6-bit Operand1 field to get 1110_xxxxxx where xxxxxx is a 6-bit extended opcode. Operand2 field is the single address. This gives 2^6 = 64 single-address instruction codes.

We need 127 single-address instructions. 64 < 127, so we also use prefix 1111 for additional single-address instructions. Using 1111_xxxxxx for Operand1 as extended opcode gives 64 more patterns. But we must reserve some 1111 patterns for zero-address.

Use patterns 1111_000000 through 1111_111110 (63 patterns) for single-address instructions. Total single-address = 64 + 63 = 127. Exactly enough!
steps.note: Pattern 1111_111111 is reserved as the prefix for zero-address instructions.

steps.title: Allocate zero-address instruction opcodes
steps.formula_raw: Zero-address capacity = 2^6 = 64 (using Operand2 field as extended opcode after 1111_111111 prefix)
steps.apply:
The pattern 1111_111111 (4-bit opcode = 1111, Operand1 = 111111) serves as the prefix for zero-address instructions. The Operand2 field (6 bits) then provides the final opcode extension, giving 2^6 = 64 zero-address instruction codes.

We need 60 zero-address instructions. 64 >= 60. This fits.
steps.note: 4 of the 64 zero-address code slots are unused, which is acceptable.

steps.title: Summary verification
steps.apply:
Double-address: 14 codes available, 14 needed. OK.
Single-address: 64 (prefix 1110) + 63 (prefix 1111, patterns 000000 to 111110) = 127 codes. Exactly 127 needed. OK.
Zero-address: 64 codes (prefix 1111_111111, then 6-bit operand2). 60 needed. OK (64 >= 60).

All instruction types can be encoded — YES, it is possible.
steps.note: The expanding opcode scheme works precisely here because the numbers are carefully chosen to fit the available bit budget.

solution.result: Yes, it is possible to encode all 14 double-address, 127 single-address, and 60 zero-address instructions using expanding opcode in the given 4+6+6 bit instruction format.

questions.formula_note: Expanding opcode technique: reserve certain 4-bit patterns as expansion prefixes; extend into Operand1 and Operand2 fields to create longer opcodes for instructions with fewer operands. Total capacity: 14 (double) + 127 (single) + 60 (zero) = 201 instructions encoded.
